content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

May 4, 1979


Anthony C. Bennetti
Deputy City Attorney
151 West Mission Street
San Jose, CA  95110

Dear Mr. Bennetti:

Thank you for your letter on behalf of Councilmember
Jerry Estruth requesting confirmation of advice rendered
to your office regarding Mr. Estruth's obligations under
the disqualification provisions of the Political Reform
Act.

The facts as we understood them were that Mr. Estruth
is employed as a broker for Dean Witter Reynolds.
Mr. Estruth handled the stock of an engineer-developer who
is active in real estate development in San Jose. Mr. Estruth
has received commissions in excess of $250 from this account.
In November of 1978, the account was transferred to another
broker in the office, and Mr. Estruth no longer receives
commissions of the account.

Government Code Section 87100 provides that a public
official cannot participate in governmental decisions in
which he or she has a financial interest. Section 87103
provides that:

An official has a financial interest within
the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally, on:
***
(c) Any source of income, other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the regular
course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value received by or
promised to the public official within twelve
months prior to the time when the decision is made;...

Mr. Anthony C. Bennetti                                    Page Two
May 4, 1979


     Mr. Estruth has received $250 from the engineer-developer
within the last twelve months thereupon, the engineer-developer
is a source of income to him for the purposes of Government
Code Sections 87100 and 87103. Based on the facts provided
the engineer-developer will continue to be a source of income
to Mr. Estruth until November 1979. Because the engineer-developer is a source of income to Mr. Estruth, he must refrain
from participating or using his official position to influence any governmental decisions that may materially affect
the engineer-developer in a manner distinguishable from the
public generally.

     Given that engineer-developer functions as a business
entity, the following standards should be applied in determining whether the engineer-developer will be materially
affected by a governmental decision:

     (1)  The effect of the decision will be to increase
          or decrease the annualized gross income of
          the engineer-developer by one percent or
          more, or the annual net income of the engineer-developer by five tenths of one percent
          (0.5%) or more;

     (2)  The effect of the decision will be to increase or decrease the assets or liabilities
          of the engineer-developer by $50,000 or
          more, or by five tenths of one percent
          (0.5%) of his current assets or liabilities,
          whichever is less.
                                   See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702

     Commission regulation 18702 also provides that: In
cases where the foregoing standards are not feasible to apply,
the financial effect of a governmental decision on a financial interest of Mr. Estruth is material if the decision
will have a substantial rather than insignificant effect on
the engineer-developer.

     I hope that our advice is helpful. If you have any
questions regarding this matter, please do not hesitate to
contact me.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Dwight E. Dickerson
                                        Legal Counsel
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division

DED:nrw

OFFICE OF

CITY ATTORNEY
CITY OF SAN JOSE

ROBERT J. LOGAN
CITY ATTORNEY

151 WEST MISSION STREET
SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA 95110
(408) 277-4454

April 12, 1979

California Fair Political Practices
Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Attn: Dwight Dickerson, Esq.

Re: Councilperson Jerry Estruth

Dear Mr. Dickerson:

Mr. Estruth has asked me to confirm the conclusions reached in a
series of phone calls among yourself, Diane Lee and myself concerning
Mr. Estruth's obligations under the Political Reform Act relating to
a source of income.

As you will recall, Mr. Estruth works for the brokerage firm of
Dean Witter Reynolds in Palo Alto. He was elected to the San Jose
City Council in November of 1978, and took office on January 1, 1979.
Up until some time in November of 1978, Mr. Estruth handled the stock
account of an engineer-developer who is quite active in real estate
development in San Jose, both on his own behalf and on behalf of others.
In November, Mr. Estruth turned the stock account over to another broker
in the firm and received his last share of commissions on said account
in an amount exceeding $250.00.

With respect to the disqualification provisions of the Act, our
conclusions may be summarized as follows:

1. Mr. Estruth has a financial interest in a governmental
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the engineer-
developer from whom he received $250.00 or more within
twelve months prior to the time when the decision is made.

2. The engineer-developer is a source of income for Mr. Estruth.

3. Mr. Estruth must refrain from making, participating in the
making, or in any way attempting to use his official position to influence any governmental decisions which he knows
or has reason to know would have any of the following effects
on the engineer-developer, or on Mr. Estruth:

California Fair Political Practices
Commission
Page 2
April 12, 1979


A.  The engineer-developer would be affected in any of the
    following ways:

    (1)  The effect of the decision will be to increase
         or decrease the annualized gross income of the
         engineer-developer by one percent or more, or
         the annual net income of the engineer-developer
         by five tenths of one percent (0.5%) or more;

    (2)  The effect of the decision will be to increase
         or decrease the assets or liabilities of the
         engineer-developer by $50,000.00 or more, or
         by five tenths of one percent (0.5%) of his
         current assets or liabilities, whichever is less.

B.  In cases where the foregoing standards are not feasible
    to apply, the financial effect of a governmental decision
    on a financial interest of Mr. Estruth is material if
    the decision will have a substantial rather than insigni-
    ficant effect on the engineer-developer.

Again, thank you for your assitance in this matter, and if this does
not reflect your understanding of our discussions, please contact the
undersigned.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        ROBERT J. LOGAN
                                        City Attorney

                                   By [signature]
                                        ANTHONY C. BENNETTI
                                        Deputy City Attorney

RJL:ACB:gm

sections.conclusion: Mr. Estruth has a financial interest in governmental decisions involving the engineer-developer (a source of income) and must refrain from participating in or influencing any governmental decisions that would materially affect the engineer-developer. Material effect is determined by: (1) increase/decrease in annualized gross income by 1% or more (or net income by 0.5% or more), or (2) increase/decrease in assets/liabilities by $50,000 or more (or 0.5% of current assets/liabilities, whichever is less). This obligation continues until November 1979 when the 12-month period expires.
sections.question_synthetic: What are Councilmember Jerry Estruth's obligations under the Political Reform Act's disqualification provisions regarding his financial interest in an engineer-developer from whom he received broker commissions exceeding $250?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Councilmember Estruth must disqualify himself from governmental decisions affecting the engineer-developer under Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103, as the engineer-developer qualifies as a source of income for purposes of disqualification. The disqualification requirement applies to decisions with material financial effects as defined by regulatory standards, and continues through November 1979.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains two letters: initial request from City Attorney (April 12, 1979) and FPPC response from Dwight E. Dickerson (May 4, 1979). The question and conclusion sections are embedded in narrative form rather than explicitly labeled. The parsing notes indicated no section headers, but the FPPC response letter clearly articulates the legal analysis and conclusion. Confidence reduced due to lack of explicit Q&A formatting and document dual-letter structure.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are Councilmember Jerry Estruth's obligations under the Political Reform Act's disqualification provisions regarding his financial interest in an engineer-developer from whom he received broker commissions exceeding $250?

CONCLUSION: Mr. Estruth has a financial interest in governmental decisions involving the engineer-developer (a source of income) and must refrain from participating in or influencing any governmental decisions that would materially affect the engineer-developer. Material effect is determined by: (1) increase/decrease in annualized gross income by 1% or more (or net income by 0.5% or more), or (2) increase/decrease in assets/liabilities by $50,000 or more (or 0.5% of current assets/liabilities, whichever is less). This obligation continues until November 1979 when the 12-month period expires.

Mr. Estruth handled the stock of an engineer-developer who is active in real estate development in San Jose. Mr. Estruth has received commissions in excess of $250 from this account. In November of 1978, the account was transferred to another broker in the office, and Mr. Estruth no longer receives commissions from the account. Government Code Section 87100 provides that a public official cannot participate in governmental decisions in which he or she has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that: An official has a financial interest within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on: (c) Any source of income, other than loans by a commercial lending institution in the regular course of business, aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value received by or promised to the public official within twelve months prior to the time when the decision is made;... Based on the facts provided the engineer-developer will continue to be a source of income to Mr. Estruth until November 1979. Because the engineer-developer is a source of income to Mr. Estruth, he must refrain from participating or using his official position to influence any governmental decisions that may materially affect the engineer-developer in a manner distinguishable from the public generally. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 Commission regulation 18702 also provides that: In cases where the foregoing standards are not feasible to apply, the financial effect of a governmental decision on a financial interest of Mr. Estruth is material if the decision will have a substantial rather than insignificant effect on the engineer-developer. embedding.summary: FPPC provides advice to San Jose City Attorney confirming that Councilmember Jerry Estruth must disqualify himself from governmental decisions materially affecting an engineer-developer who is a source of income (having paid him $250+ in broker commissions), with specific materiality thresholds based on percentage changes in income, assets, or liabilities.
